fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Further closure properties of truncation levels

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: If A is a proposition and B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} is family of propositions, then the dependent sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} is a proposition.

We fix pairs \mathopen {} \left ( a,b \right ) \mathclose {} and \mathopen {} \left ( a',b' \right ) \mathclose {} in \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}. By via , it suffices to exhibit identifications p \colon a = a' and q \colon p_* b = b'. These are given respectively by our assumptions that A and Ba' are propositions.

If A is contractible and B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} is a family of contractible types, then the dependent sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx is contractible.

Let a_0 be the centre of contraction of A and let b_0 be the centre of contraction of Ba_0. Then we let the centre of contraction of \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx be \mathopen {} \left ( a_0,b_0 \right ) \mathclose {}. The necessary identifications are obtained from the contractibility of A and Ba_0 by the characterisation of identifications in dependent sums (, ).

Let A be n-truncated and let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of n-truncated types. Then the dependent sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx is n-truncated.

By induction on n, with the base cases following from and . In the inductive step, we need to show that for all u,v \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx the identification type u=v is n+1-truncated assuming the same of all the identification types of A and Bx for x:A. By , u=v is a retract of \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p:u.1=v.1 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p:u.1=v.1 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p:u.1=v.1 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p:u.1=v.1 \right ) \mathclose {} } } p_*u.2=v.2 and the latter is n+1-truncated by our inductive hypothesis; thus the result follows from .

Let A be an arbitrary type and let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of n-truncated types. Then the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx is n-truncated.

By induction on n, with the base cases following from and respectively. In the inductive step, we need to show that for all f,g \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx the identification type f=g is n+1-truncated assuming the same for the identification types of each Bx. By , f=g is a retract of \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx=gx and the latter is n+1-truncated by our inductive hypothesis. Therefore, the result follows from .
It is a proposition that a given type A is contractible.

Suppose we have two centres of contraction a,a':A with \chi _ \bullet \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } a = x and \chi '_ \bullet \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } a'=x as witnesses of contraction. To identify \mathopen {} \left ( a, \chi _ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {} with \mathopen {} \left ( a', \chi '_ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {}, we employ the covariant sum of each of the pointwise path object structures on \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } -=x over the intrinsic path object structure on A, constructing an identification p \colon a = a' followed by a homotopy H \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \mathopen {} \left ( \chi _{a'} \right ) \mathclose {} _*^{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } -=x} \chi _ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {} \sim \chi '_ \bullet. Fixing z \colon A, we compute:
The first and second steps follow from general computation lemmas for transport, and the last step follows from the fact that A is a set, which we may deduce from and .

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being a proposition is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: It is a proposition that a given type A is a proposition.

To identify two witnesses \phi , \phi ' \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } x = y that A is a proposition, it suffices via to exhibit a double homotopy in A identifying each \phi xy with \phi 'xy. By , this follows from our assumption that A is a proposition.
It is a proposition that a given type A is n-truncated.
We proceed by induction on the truncation level n.

fr:ol.fr:li: When n=-2, we recall from that being contractible is a proposition.
When n=-1, we recall from that being a proposition is a proposition.

For n \geq 0, we assume by induction that being n-1-truncated is a proposition to prove that being n-truncated is a proposition. In other words, we must show that \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {isTruncated} \, \mathopen {} \left ( n-1 \right ) \mathclose {} \, A is a proposition. By our inductive hypothesis, this is a dependent product of propositions ().

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being an n-truncated map is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: That a given map f \colon A \to B is n-truncated is a proposition.

We must check that the product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {isTruncated} \, n \, \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, x \right ) \mathclose {} is a proposition; indeed, this is a product of propositions () as being an n-truncated type is a proposition ().

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being a surjection is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: That a given map f \colon E \to B is surjective is a proposition.
By .

Let A be a contractible type with centre a. For any family of types B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type}, we may define a function \mathsf {ind}_{ A,a } \colon B a \to \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x : A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x : A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x : A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x : A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx satisfying \mathsf {ind}_{ A,a } \, b \, a = b.

Let \chi _x \colon a = x be the witness of contraction for any x:A. Given b : Ba and x:A, we define \mathsf {ind}_{ A,a } \, b \, x : Bx by transporting b along the identification \chi _a ^{-1} \cdot \chi _x \colon a = x:
Then we compute \mathsf {ind}_{ A,a } \, b \, a \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \chi _a ^{-1} \cdot \chi _a \right ) \mathclose {} _* b = \mathsf {refl} _*b = b.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path objects and the univalence axiom

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A reflexive graph \mathcal {A} \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} , \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } , \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a type \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} of vertices together with a family of types \mathopen {} \left ( \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } \right ) \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Type} of edges and an assignment \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } x of reflexivity data to each vertex.

Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph. A displayed reflexive graph \mathcal {B} \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} \right \rvert \mathclose {} , \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ \bullet } , \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} }^{ \bullet } \right ) \mathclose {} over \mathcal {A} is given by families of types \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Type} and \mathopen {} \left ( \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ \bullet } \right ) \mathclose {} : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \} \mathclose {} } } x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \to Bx \to By \to \mathsf {Type} together with an assignment of displayed reflexivity data \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} }^{ x } {u} : b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} } b for every u:Bx.

Given a displayed reflexive graph \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A} and a vertex x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {}, we shall write \mathcal {B} \mathopen {} \left [ x \right ] \mathclose {} for the component of \mathcal {B} at x:A, which we define to be the following reflexive graph:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} \mathopen {} \left [ x \right ] \mathclose {} \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} \right \rvert \mathclose {} x \\ u \approx _{ \mathcal {B} \mathopen {} \left [ x \right ] \mathclose {} } v &: \equiv u \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} } v \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} \mathopen {} \left [ x \right ] \mathclose {} } {u} &: \equiv \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} }^{ x } {u} \end {aligned}

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed reflexive graph over \mathcal {A}. The total reflexive graph of \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A}, written \mathcal {A} . \mathcal {B}, is defined as follows:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} . \mathcal {B} \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} \right \rvert \mathclose {} x \\ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathcal {A} . \mathcal {B} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p:x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p:x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p:x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p:x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} } } u \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ p } v \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} . \mathcal {B} } \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x}, \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} }^{ x } {u} \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic reflexive graph on a type

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The intrinsic reflexive graph with vertices in a type A, written \mathord { \vartriangle } A, is defined by setting x \approx _{ \mathord { \vartriangle } A } y : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( x = y \right ) \mathclose {} and \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathord { \vartriangle } A } {x} : \equiv \mathsf {refl}.

Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph, and let B \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the dependent sum projection \pi _1 \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx \right ) \mathclose {} \to A creates a reflexive graph structure \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} on \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx as follows:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx \\ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } x \end {aligned}

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two reflexive graphs. We define the binary product of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} to be the following reflexive graph:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \times \mathcal {B} \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \times \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \\ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathcal {A} \times \mathcal {B} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,u \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} \times \mathopen {} \left ( u \approx _{ \mathcal {B} } v \right ) \mathclose {} \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} \times \mathcal {B} } \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x}, \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} } {u} \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Product of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {B} be a family of reflexive graphs indexed in a type A. We define the product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathcal {B} x of the family \mathcal {B} to be the following reflexive graph:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {} \\ f \approx _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathcal {B} x } g &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } f x \approx _{ \mathcal {B} x } g x \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathcal {B} x } {f} &: \equiv \lambda x \mathpunct {.} \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} x } \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A reflexive graph \mathcal {A} is called univalent when for all x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y is a proposition, or (equivalently), is contractible with centre \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} \right ) \mathclose {}. We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.

Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let B \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the reflexive subgraph comprehension \mathopen {} \left \{ x: \mathcal {A} \, \middle \vert \, B \mathopen {} \left ( x \right ) \mathclose {} \right \} \mathclose {} is a path object.
Univalence follows from univalence of \mathcal {A} and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic path object on a type

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The intrinsic reflexive graph on a type A is a path object.
Univalence follows immediately by identification induction.
The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The reflexive graph structure on \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} by homotopy and identity homotopies is univalent.

Given a dependent function f : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, if each component Bx is contractible with centre fx then the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} is contractible with centre f.
This follows immediately from univalence for functions.
The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.

Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of propositions; fixing f : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, it suffices to show any other g : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx may be identified with f. As B is a family of propositions, we know that each Bx is contractible with centre fx. Therefore, the closure of contractible types under dependent products () shows that \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx is contractible with centre f.
The product of a family \mathcal {B} \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} path objects is a path object.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products () via the distributivity of \prod over \sum.
Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two path objects; then the binary product \mathcal {A} \times \mathcal {B} is a path object.
The univalence property of \mathcal {A} \times \mathcal {B} follows immediately from that of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B}.

We have seen in how to obtain principles for equational reasoning by imposing path object structures on types; in general, we will need an analogous tool for working with families of types B \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Type} given a path object \mathcal {A}. Such a tool must account for heterogeneous identifications in B, i.e. identifications between elements of different fibers of B; in this section, we explore three possible variations on the concept of “dependent path object”:

fr:ol.fr:li: displayed path objects provide an unbiased and heterogeneous account of identifications between elements of different fibers of B;
left-cloven families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a forward transport operation;
right-cloven families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a backward transport operation.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: All of these notions are useful: because it is unbiased (i.e. prefers neither forward nor backward transport), the displayed representation is the most fundamental; on the other hand, the biased right- and left-cloven representations are the most frequently used. The fact that the latter are described directly for families of path objects in the existing sense is practically important for the compositionality of reasoning in univalent foundations, as we will more frequently have general-purpose building blocks for composing path objects than for composing displayed path objects.

A displayed reflexive graph \mathcal {B} over a reflexive graph \mathcal {A} is called univalent when for each x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} the component \mathcal {B} \mathopen {} \left [ x \right ] \mathclose {} is univalent. We define a displayed path object to be a univalent displayed reflexive graph.
We will write \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, \mathcal {A} for the (large) type of displayed path objects over \mathcal {A}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The total path object of a displayed path object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed path object over \mathcal {A}. Then the total reflexive graph \mathcal {A} . \mathcal {B} is a path object.
Univalence is established in two singleton inductions, first using univalence of \mathcal {A} followed by univalence of \mathcal {B}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let \mathcal {B} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a family of path objects. A left cleaving of \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A} is given by the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: a pushforward operation assigning to each edge p \colon x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y a map p_* \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} y \right \rvert \mathclose {};

a family \Phi _{ \mathcal {B} } \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} \right ) \mathclose {} _*b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} x } b witnessing the unit law that pushforward along the reflexivity datum \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} \colon x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } x is the identity function.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object and let \mathcal {B} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a family of path objects. A right cleaving of \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A} is defined by the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: a pullback operation assigning to each edge p \colon x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y a map p^* \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} y \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {};

a family \Phi _{ \mathcal {B} } \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} x } \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x}^*b witnessing the unit law that pullback along the reflexivity datum \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} \colon x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } x is the identity function.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Given a type A and a family of path objects \mathcal {B} \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, we can always define a left cleaving of \mathcal {B} over the intrinsic path object \mathord { \vartriangle } A. The left cleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.
We next show how right-cloven and left-cloven families of path objects both give rise to appropriate displayed path objects on their base.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Display of left-cloven families of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object. Any left-cloven family of path objects \mathcal {B} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over \mathcal {A} in which:

fr:ol.fr:li: for p \colon x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y, we define b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ p } b' to be p_*b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} y } b';

for x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} and b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {}, the identity element \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} } {x} \colon b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} } b is given by the component \Phi _{ \mathcal {B} } xb \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} \right ) \mathclose {} _*b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} x } {b} of the unit law of the given right cleaving of \mathcal {B}.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Univalence is easily established using the univalence of each \mathcal {B} x.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Display of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object. Any right-cloven family of path objects \mathcal {B} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over \mathcal {A} in which:

fr:ol.fr:li: for p \colon x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y, we define b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ p } b' to be b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} y } p^*b';

for x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} and b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {}, the reflexivity datum \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} }^{ x } {b} \colon b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} } b is given by the component \Phi _{ \mathcal {B} }xb \colon b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} x } \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x}^*b of the unit law of the given cleaving of \mathcal {B}.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Univalence is more difficult to establish than for the case of left cleavings (). Fixing a: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {}, we must show that the reflexive graph structure on \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} with edges in b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} a } \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {a}^*b' and reflexivity data for b:Ba given by \Phi _B ab \colon b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} a } \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {a}^*b is univalent.

It suffices to show that the type of singletons \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} a } \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {a}^*b' is a proposition. As each \mathcal {B} a is univalent, this is easily shown to be a retract of \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} a } b', which is a proposition again by the univalence of \mathcal {B} a. Therefore, we conclude via .

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Covariant sum of a left-cloven family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {B} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a left-cloven family of path objects over a path object \mathcal {A}. We define the covariant sum of the family \mathcal {B}, written \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathcal {A} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathcal {A} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathcal {A} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathcal {A} \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathcal {B} x}, to be the total path object of the displayed path object associated to \mathcal {B} by . In particular, we have defined \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathcal {A} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathcal {A} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathcal {A} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathcal {A} \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathcal {B} x} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} } } p_*u \approx _{ \mathcal {B} y } v.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Contravariant sum of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {B} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} be an right-cloven family of path objects over a path object \mathcal {A}. We define the contravariant sum of the family \mathcal {B}, written \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathcal {A} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathcal {A} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathcal {A} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathcal {A} \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathcal {B} x}, to be the total path object of the displayed path object associated to \mathcal {B} as in . In particular, we have defined \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx } \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y \right ) \mathclose {} } } u \approx _{ \mathcal {B} x } p^*v.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path object structure on reflexive graphs

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We may define a path object structure on the type of reflexive graphs with vertices in a given type A, so that an isomorphism from \mathcal {G} to \mathcal {G} ' is given by a pair of a pointwise equivalence of types H \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( x \approx _{ \mathcal {G} } y \right ) \mathclose {} \cong \mathopen {} \left ( x \approx _{ \mathcal {G} ' } y \right ) \mathclose {} that preserves reflexivity data as witnessed by a dependent homotopy I \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} H \, x \, x \, \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {G} } {x} \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} \sim \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {G} ' }.

We first show that the dependent product of intrinsic path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( -xx \right ) \mathclose {} is a left-cloven over the path object \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type}: given an equivalence H : \mathopen {} \left ( \approx _{ \mathcal {G} } \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} } \mathopen {} \left ( \approx _{ \mathcal {G} ' } \right ) \mathclose {} of graphs and a reflexivity datum r \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \approx _{ \mathcal {G} } x, we define H_*r x : \equiv H \, x \, x \, \mathopen {} \left ( rx \right ) \mathclose {}; the unit law holds definitionally.

Therefore, we may form the covariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( E: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( E: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( E: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( E: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( Exx \right ) \mathclose {}, in which equivalences are given by homotopies of reflexivity data over graph equivalences.

Given f \colon A \to B and b:B, we shall impose a path object structure on the fiber \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx=b of f over b. The family \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( f-=b \right ) \mathclose {} \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} given by intrinsic path objects in each component is right-cloven over \mathord { \vartriangle } A:

fr:ol.fr:li: for p \colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b \colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {act}_{ f } p \cdot \phi;
the unit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Therefore, we may compute the contravariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( fx=b \right ) \mathclose {} } using the cleaving above, with \mathopen {} \left ( x, \phi \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( fx=b \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( y, \psi \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p: x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p: x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p: x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p: x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } } \phi = \mathsf {act}_{ f } p \cdot \psi.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f \colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon s \sim s' \colon B \to A and a witness h \colon f \circ s' \sim 1_{ B } that s' is a section of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon f \circ s \sim 1_{ B } that s is a section of f as follows:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: H^*h x : \equiv \mathsf {act}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( Hx \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: As we evidently have \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A } {s} \right ) \mathclose {} ^*h x = \mathsf {act}_{ f } \mathsf {refl} \cdot h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of f \circ- \sim 1_{ B } over the path object \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies in . In this sense, sectionhood witnesses are right-cloven over their underlying maps.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f \colon B \to A be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon r \sim r' \colon A \to B and a witness h \colon r' \circ f \sim 1_{ B } that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon r \circ f \sim 1_{ B } that r is a retraction of f as follows:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: H^*h x : \equiv H \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: As we have \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A } r \right ) \mathclose {} ^*h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of - \circ f \sim 1_{ A } over \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies in . Thus retractionhood witnesses constitute are right-cloven over underlying maps.

A suitable path object on sections to a fixed function r \colon A \to B is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as sectionhood witnesses form a right-cloven family over their underlying sections (). In particular, an isomorphism of sections of r from \mathopen {} \left ( s,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( s',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_s \colon s \sim s' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} \mathsf {act}_{ r } \mathopen {} \left ( H_s x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

A suitable path object on retractions to a fixed function s \colon B \to A is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } B \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as retractionhood witnesses form a right-cloven family over their underlying sections (). In particular, an isomorphism of retractions of s from \mathopen {} \left ( r,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( r',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_r \colon r \sim r' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} H_r \mathopen {} \left ( r x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Further closure properties of truncation levels

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: If A is a proposition and B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} is family of propositions, then the dependent sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} is a proposition.

We fix pairs \mathopen {} \left ( a,b \right ) \mathclose {} and \mathopen {} \left ( a',b' \right ) \mathclose {} in \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}. By via , it suffices to exhibit identifications p \colon a = a' and q \colon p_* b = b'. These are given respectively by our assumptions that A and Ba' are propositions.

If A is contractible and B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} is a family of contractible types, then the dependent sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx is contractible.

Let a_0 be the centre of contraction of A and let b_0 be the centre of contraction of Ba_0. Then we let the centre of contraction of \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx be \mathopen {} \left ( a_0,b_0 \right ) \mathclose {}. The necessary identifications are obtained from the contractibility of A and Ba_0 by the characterisation of identifications in dependent sums (, ).

Let A be n-truncated and let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of n-truncated types. Then the dependent sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx is n-truncated.

By induction on n, with the base cases following from and . In the inductive step, we need to show that for all u,v \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx the identification type u=v is n+1-truncated assuming the same of all the identification types of A and Bx for x:A. By , u=v is a retract of \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p:u.1=v.1 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p:u.1=v.1 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p:u.1=v.1 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p:u.1=v.1 \right ) \mathclose {} } } p_*u.2=v.2 and the latter is n+1-truncated by our inductive hypothesis; thus the result follows from .

Let A be an arbitrary type and let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of n-truncated types. Then the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx is n-truncated.

By induction on n, with the base cases following from and respectively. In the inductive step, we need to show that for all f,g \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx the identification type f=g is n+1-truncated assuming the same for the identification types of each Bx. By , f=g is a retract of \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx=gx and the latter is n+1-truncated by our inductive hypothesis. Therefore, the result follows from .
It is a proposition that a given type A is contractible.

Suppose we have two centres of contraction a,a':A with \chi _ \bullet \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } a = x and \chi '_ \bullet \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } a'=x as witnesses of contraction. To identify \mathopen {} \left ( a, \chi _ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {} with \mathopen {} \left ( a', \chi '_ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {}, we employ the covariant sum of each of the pointwise path object structures on \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } -=x over the intrinsic path object structure on A, constructing an identification p \colon a = a' followed by a homotopy H \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \mathopen {} \left ( \chi _{a'} \right ) \mathclose {} _*^{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } -=x} \chi _ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {} \sim \chi '_ \bullet. Fixing z \colon A, we compute:
The first and second steps follow from general computation lemmas for transport, and the last step follows from the fact that A is a set, which we may deduce from and .

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being a proposition is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: It is a proposition that a given type A is a proposition.

To identify two witnesses \phi , \phi ' \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } x = y that A is a proposition, it suffices via to exhibit a double homotopy in A identifying each \phi xy with \phi 'xy. By , this follows from our assumption that A is a proposition.
It is a proposition that a given type A is n-truncated.
We proceed by induction on the truncation level n.

fr:ol.fr:li: When n=-2, we recall from that being contractible is a proposition.
When n=-1, we recall from that being a proposition is a proposition.

For n \geq 0, we assume by induction that being n-1-truncated is a proposition to prove that being n-truncated is a proposition. In other words, we must show that \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {isTruncated} \, \mathopen {} \left ( n-1 \right ) \mathclose {} \, A is a proposition. By our inductive hypothesis, this is a dependent product of propositions ().

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being an n-truncated map is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: That a given map f \colon A \to B is n-truncated is a proposition.

We must check that the product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {isTruncated} \, n \, \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, x \right ) \mathclose {} is a proposition; indeed, this is a product of propositions () as being an n-truncated type is a proposition ().

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being a surjection is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: That a given map f \colon E \to B is surjective is a proposition.
By .
Any invertible map f \colon A \to B is contractible.

Let \epsilon \colon f \circ f ^{-1} = 1_{ B } be the counit of the inverse to f \colon A \to B. Given b:B, we must show that the fiber of f over b is contractible; the centre of contraction is given by the pair \mathopen {} \left ( f ^{-1} x, \epsilon x \right ) \mathclose {}, and the required paths can be constructed via the univalence principle for fibers using the unit \eta \colon f ^{-1} \circ f = 1_{ A }, the coherence \chi \colon \mathsf {act}_{ f } \circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f, and the naturality of homotopies.
Any contractible map f \colon A \to B is a section.

Let f \colon A \to B be contractible; we define a retraction r \colon B \to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. To construct h_r \colon r \circ f \sim 1_{ A } exhibiting r as a retraction of f, we use the identification between any two elements of the fiber of f over fx.
Any contractible map f \colon A \to B is a retraction.

Let f \colon A \to B be contractible; we define a section s \colon B \to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. The witness h_s \colon f \circ s \sim 1_{ B } exhibiting s as a section of f, we use the second component of the centre of contraction.
Any contractible map f \colon A \to B is a biinvertible.
Because contractible maps are both sections and retractions.
Any quasi-invertible map f \colon A \to B is contractible.
Any quasi-inverse can be improved to a coherent inverse, and any coherently invertible map is contractible.
Any biinvertible map f \colon A \to B is contractible.
As biinvertible maps are quasi-invertible and quasi-invertible maps are contractible.

Let f \colon A \to B be biinvertible; then for any type C, the postcomposition map \mathopen {} \left ( f \circ - \right ) \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {} \left ( C \to A \right ) \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {} \left ( C \to B \right ) \mathclose {} is biinvertible.

The section and retraction are built using by postcomposing with the designated section and retraction of f; the witnesses for the postcomposition sections and retractions are constructed using the univalence principle for function spaces.

Let f \colon A \to B be biinvertible; then for any type C, the precomposition map \mathopen {} \left ( - \circ f \right ) \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {} \left ( B \to C \right ) \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {} \left ( A \to C \right ) \mathclose {} is biinvertible.
Analogous to the corresponding result for pushforward along biinvertible maps.
Any biinvertible map f \colon A \to B is propositional.
We must show that each fiber of f is a proposition; in fact, each fiber is contractible as biinvertible maps are contractible, and contractible types are propositions.
Any biinvertible map f \colon A \to B is surjective.
This is immediate, using the unit map of propositional truncation and the section of f.
Any biinvertible map f \colon A \to B is a bijection.
By and .
Any embedding f \colon A \to B is propositional.

We must check that for every b:B, the fiber of f at b is a proposition. Fixing u_0,u_1: \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, b we must check that u_0=u_1. We let \mathopen {} \left ( a_0,p_0 \right ) \mathclose {} \equiv u_0 and \mathopen {} \left ( a_1,p_1 \right ) \mathclose {} \equiv u_1 so that we have p_0 \colon fa_0=b and p_1 \colon fa_1=b. Invoking the path object structure on fibers (), it suffices to exhibit the following data:
As f is an embedding, the map \mathsf {act}_{ f } \colon a_0 = a_1 \to fa_0 = fa_1 has an inverse \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {act}_{ f } ^{-1} , \epsilon , \eta , \chi \right ) \mathclose {}.
Let \alpha \colon fa_0 = fa_1 be the concatenation p_0 \cdot p_1 ^{-1}. Therefore we may define:

To define \psi \colon p_0= \mathsf {act}_{ f } \phi \cdot p_1 we use the counit of our inverse as we have \epsilon \alpha \colon \mathsf {act}_{ f } \phi = p_0 \cdot p_1 ^{-1}. Thus concatenating on either side with p_1 we have our goal, modulo the laws of identification concatenation and symmetry respectively.
Any propositional map f \colon A \to B is an embedding.

Fixing x,y:A we must show that \mathsf {act}_{ f } \colon x = y \to fx=fy is invertible. By and , it suffices to exhibit a biinverse to \mathsf {act}_{ f } (it is convenient that the underlying maps of the section and retraction need not be identical).

We first define the sections s_{x,y} \colon fx = fy \to x = y. Because f is assumed to be propositional, we have by an assignment \phi _{x,y}: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( h:fx=fy \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( h:fx=fy \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( h:fx=fy \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( h:fx=fy \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,h ^{-1} \right ) \mathclose {}. We therefore define s_{x,y}h : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \phi _{x,y}h \right ) \mathclose {} .1; the witness of sectionhood can be constructed using \mathopen {} \left ( \phi _{x,y}h \right ) \mathclose {} .2 modulo some identification algebra.

To construct the retractions r_{x,y} \colon fx=fy \to x = y, we note by our assumption that we have an assignment \psi _{x,y}: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( h:fx=fy \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( h:fx=fy \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( h:fx=fy \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( h:fx=fy \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {} =_{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,h ^{-1} \right ) \mathclose {}, and we define r_{x,y}h : \equiv \mathsf {act}_{ -.1 } \mathopen {} \left ( \psi _{x,y}h \right ) \mathclose {}. It remains to show that for all x,y:A and h \colon x =y, we have \mathsf {act}_{ -.1 } \mathopen {} \left ( \psi _{x,y}h \right ) \mathclose {} =h. By induction on h it suffices to show that for all x:A we have \mathsf {act}_{ -.1 } \mathopen {} \left ( \psi _{x,x} \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {} = \mathsf {refl}. We will deduce that \psi _{x,x} \mathsf {refl} = \mathsf {refl} from our assumption that f \colon A \to B is propositional and thus fibred in sets via .
Any biinvertible map is an embedding.
By and .
Any bijection f \colon A \to B is contractible.

Fixing b:B, we must check that the fiber of f over b is contractible. For the centre of contraction, we must construct an element of this fiber; because f is propositional by , the fiber is a proposition, so it suffices by the induction principle for propositional truncation to observe that the fiber is inhabited because f is surjective.
Any bijection f \colon A \to B is biinvertible.
By , bijections are contractible; by , bijections are contractible.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Coherence of different notions of equivalence

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f \colon A \to B be a section of some r \colon B \to A; the type of sections of f is, then, a proposition.

Using the univalence principle for dependent function spaces, we can see that the type of sections of f \colon A \to B is a retract of the fiber of f \circ - at 1_{ B }, using the univalence principle for sections.

To show that the type of sections of f is a proposition, we may assume beforehand that we have such a section of f; therefore, because we have assumed a retraction of f already, we know that f is biinvertible and therefore that f is contractible. By , it suffices to show that the fiber of f \circ - at 1_{ B } is a proposition, which follows via and .
Let f \colon A \to B be a retraction of some s \colon B \to A; the type of retractions of f is, then, a proposition.
Analogous to the corresponding result for sections of sections, using the fact that the type of retractions of f can be seen to be a retract of the fiber of - \circ f at 1_{ A }.
The type of biinverses of a given function f \colon A \to B is a proposition.
Sections of sections and retractions of retractions are unique.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The coherent remainder of a section

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Given a function f \colon A \to B and a section g \colon B \to A witnessed by \epsilon \colon f \circ g = 1_{ B }, we define the coherent remainder of \mathopen {} \left ( g, \epsilon \right ) \mathclose {} to be the following sum:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( g, \epsilon \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {act}_{ f } \circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: For any f \colon A \to B, it is evident that the type of inverses to f is (up to reassociation) precisely the dependent sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {section} \, f \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {section} \, f \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {section} \, f \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {section} \, f \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f }.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Coherent remainder as a path object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f \colon A \to B be a function equipped with a section \sigma \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon B \to A, \epsilon \colon f \circ g = 1_{ B } \right ) \mathclose {}. We can equip the coherent remainder of \sigma with the structure of a path object as follows:

fr:ol.fr:li: We equip g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } with the following dependent product of intrinsic path object structures: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {}

For each \eta \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{ A }, we likewise equip \mathsf {act}_{ f } \circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f with the following dependent product of intrinsic path object structures: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {act}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( \eta x \right ) \mathclose {} = \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {}

fr:li.fr:p: We exhibit the latter as a right-cloven family of path objects over the former with the cleaving that for \eta _0, \eta _1 \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } and \eta _{01} \colon \eta _0 \sim \eta _1 defines \eta _{01}^* \colon \mathsf {act}_{ f } \circ \eta _1 \sim \epsilon \circ f \to \mathsf {act}_{ f } \circ \eta _0 \sim \epsilon \circ f as follows:

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: With this cleaving in mind, finally define \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma to be the following contravariant sum of path objects:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{
